Step 1: Snap Suggestion Part 2

Jon: 	We are onto the big promise section of this snap suggestion. If you will
recall, there were several parts to the snap suggestion. Go back and
look at the last video, if you forgot the formula.
This is the suggestion part of the equation. We’ve already covered the
pattern interrupt primes the mind for a suggestion. We didn’t cover
what that suggestion was. So, the suggestion part is pretty simple.
Your only goal here is to encourage the viewer to watch the video until
the end. You’re going to achieve this in 2 ways: number one, by
giving the viewer a suggestion while they’re in that state of pattern
interrupt. Remember, you kind of scratch the vinyl record. The brain is
open, at this point, to a suggestion. That’s how suggestion patterns
work. That’s how hypnosis works. That’s how NLP works.
So, you’re just going to give them a very benign suggestion, and then
you’re going to ethically bribe them to watch the video until the end.
What do I mean by “ethically bribe?” We’ll find out here in just a
moment.
Step one is the suggestion. All you want to do is to say, “Watch this
presentation until the end, and you will also discover.”
Remember, we set this up in the pattern interrupt. I said, “The magic
jacket is going to show you how you’re going to lose weight. Now,
watch this presentation until the end” – that’s the suggestion – “and
you’ll also discover,” and then we’ll go into what they’re going to
discover. I’ll give you that formula in just a moment.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

2
I want you to notice the use of the term “presentation.” This increased
my video sales letter conversions by 25% over the word “video.”
That’s crazy. That’s crazy.
But, I wanted to give you this tip within this part of the tutorials,
because this is where I use the word “presentation.” I wanted to make
sure you knew that tip, so try “presentation” versus “video.” Not really
sure why it’s such a powerful difference, but it is.
So, “Watch this presentation up until the end.” That’s a suggestion,
and you’re setting them up for a big promise. “And this is what you’re
also going to discover,” and what you’re going to do is you’re going to
take the benefits, you’re going to go back to one of your tips benefits,
or 2. That’s fine, too.
But here’s the key. You want to do that with 2 to 3 “withouts.”
Remember the without formula? If you don’t, that’s okay. I’m about to
cover it right now.
First of all, do not use this without trick, if you are using an avoid tip. If
one of the tips that you want to do is something like, “You’re also going
to discover the 3 foods you absolutely have to avoid,” you don’t want to
use the word “without” after that. You only want to use the word
“without” when there’s a positive benefit.
So, if I say, “You’re also going to discover 10 ways to make money
online without fill-in-the-blank,” that is the 2 to 3 withouts.
Here’s how this looks in an example. You’re about to discover how
you can burn more fat in a single day than you have in the past 15
days, without starving yourself silly or doing any cardio whatsoever. I
have 2 “withouts” there. There’s starving yourself silly and not doing
cardio.
But I have a positive, positive thing. “Burn more fat in a single day
than you have in the past 15 days,” comes right out of one of my tips.
Okay? I can pull a tip or I can pull a benefit. I can pull anything from
here. But I just used my tips for my benefits. Very, very simple. That
is a suggestion.
You can have 2 to 3 of these, what I call benefit promises. You can
have 2 to 3 in a row. So, it’s beneficial to include an avoid tip, when
you have more than one. For example, I might say, “Let’s go back
over here and I’ll show you again, how you can burn more fat in a
single day than you have in the past 15 days, without starving yourself
silly or doing any cardio whatsoever.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

3
And on the next slide, I can say, “How a certain food will help you burn
more fat at night, without costing you a penny more in your food
budget.”
I only have one without there. I just made that up off the top of my
head. That’s how easy this is, once you know the formula. That’s only
one without.
And then, I would go to the avoid without. “Plus, you’ll discover the 3
things you must absolutely avoid, if you want to blank.” For you, that
might be different than losing weight. For you, that might be making
money online, that might be a service that you provide, anything. It’s
the 3 things you want them to avoid. But notice there’s no “without”
there.
So now, you’ve made a suggestion to watch the video, and you’ve
given them this really big promise. You said, “If you watch the video
until the end,” that’s a big condition. You have to watch until the end,
“then you’re going to discover all of this stuff.”
The cool thing about this is that your viewer does not know when the
end is. So, when you say, “Watch this until the end,” and you’re going
to use that phrase at least 3 times in your VSL – I’ll show you where –
3 times, at least. “Watch until the end.” “So, keep watching.” Just a
variation of that. You will keeping opening a loop and closing a loop.
You’re going to open a loop for another tip, and you’re going to say,
“I’m going to tell you about that in a moment, just keep watching.”
They don’t know when the end is, and they will watch all the way to the
point where you desperately need them to watch, and that is the grand
offer.
If they watch and they cancel out before the grand offer, the whole
thing is moot. So, you have to get them to watch to the grand offer.
That’s why I call it the most important step in the VSL process. That’s
the big promise, pretty simple.
Then, there’s video scarcity; that’s the next. This is to get your viewer
to watch the video now. We’ve already said, “Please watch this video
until the end. You need to watch this until the end, if you’re going to
discover these 3 big, big promises that I’m talking about. But you also
have to watch this video now. You can’t watch it tomorrow or the next
day. You need to stop what you’re doing and watch this video now.”
What I want you to do here is to use real scarcity. Don’t make stuff up.
Use real scarcity. Let the viewer know that your video will simply not
be online forever, for example. That’s true. Your video won’t be online
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

4
forever. Nothing will be online forever, right? That’s one way of saying
it that creates a sense of urgency.
Other examples of this kind of wording, and I really love these, is “I
don’t know how long I’ll have this presentation online. So, watch this
now, while you can.” That’s how you end all this.
“I don’t know how long this presentation will be free of charge.” That’s
another really good one. You can say, “Look, I’ve put so much content
into this presentation on how to select the right shoes for your feet,”
you can make up anything, it doesn’t matter, “that I’m going to
eventually start charging for this, I think. I don’t know how long it will
be free; so, watch this now, while it’s still free.”
I’ve created scarcity. The video is not going to be free that much
longer, so I really need to watch this right now.
If someone is watching this going, “You know what? Maybe I should
watch this right now and not come back to it tomorrow, because it
might not be here.” That’s what you want people to be believing.
I’m going to give you a full example here. I
“I have no idea how long I will keep this presentation online, or how
long it will be free of charge to watch.”
Notice what I’ve done there. I have combined 2 scarcity elements. “I
have no idea how long it will be online, and I don’t know how long it will
be free. So, watch this now, while you still can.” That’s how I exit out
of this video scarcity element.
“Watch this now, while you can.” That’s scarcity.
Next step is something completely different. This is my Mr. Monty
Python, something completely different. This is very, very important,
and I would say it’s in the top 3 things that I see when looking at the
last 100, 150 VSL’s that I’ve studied, that people who are not having
success leave out. They leave this out.
This is especially true of the fitness and IM guys. It’s very true of the
fitness and IM guys. IM is internet marketing. I’d say it’s even more
true for internet marketing guys.
For some reason, they believe that their system or their thing is totally
unique and people get it. But guess what? They don’t. Here is what
the purpose of this is. Your video is something the viewer has never
seen before. The tendency is going to be after the pattern break
occurs and their brain starts returning back to getting into the mode of
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

5
listening. After you cover the benefits, the pattern break is pretty much
gone. A lot of viewers, at this point, will be thinking, “You know what?
This sounds just like blank.”
If it’s a weight loss offer, they might think, “This sound just like the
Atkins diet,” or “this sounds just like Body for Life,” or “I bet this is just
like blank, the offer I just watched last week.”
If it’s a service, “Ah, all dry cleaners are alike.” This is what you don’t
want them to think. This is how you get them to not think that.
You must reset their mental framework into a state of discovering
something new. Discovering is the key word here. Something new.
They’re about to discover something new.
Here’s an example of how this would play out. “You may think that
you…“ Notice I didn’t say, “I know you’re thinking.” It’s “you may
think,” remember the may tip and the or tip? “You may think you have
tried it all and seen it all, but you have never seen anything like this
before. I promise these tips and solutions will shock you.”
Now, I’m pulling out the wordsmithing patterns. Now I’m pulling out the
power words that we gave you the list for. Shock you, stun you,
surprise you, astonish you. This is how you wordsmith something.
“I promise these tips and solutions will make you happy.” That’s not
good. These tips and solutions will shock you. That stops people in
their tracks. That’s another pattern interrupt.
So, all I’ve done here is say, “You may be thinking that you’ve seen
and heard this before.”
What if it’s a service? I’m going to take an example. What if it’s a legal
service that specializes in helping people find closed cases that are
open? I’m making this up. “You may think that you’ve heard all there
is to know about the legal world and how cases are closed, but you’ve
never seen what I’m about to tell you.”
This works in any industry. Any industry at all. You can be in the
hospitality industry, you can be in the hospital industry, you can be a
fitness writer, it doesn’t matter.
The key thing is to say here that you think you’ve seen it all before, but
this is not like anything you’ve seen before, and something is going to
shock you.
“So, where it says these tips and solutions, here’s a really unique tip.
It’s not on the slides, so I want you to pay attention and write this
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

6
down.” You might want to say, “One tip in particular will totally shock
you. One tip in particular will totally shock you.”
I love using the one tip in particular, because what does that set the
mind to do? It sets the mind to look out for that one tip, right? If that
one tip is at the 18-minute mark in your video, that’s a good thing,
because that’s right before your pitch. That is something completely
different.
Then, the final step is the “let’s make a deal” transition. All you’re
doing is you’re moving the viewer into the next step, which is step 2 of
the 5-step process, the vital connection.
This is the last step of the snap suggestion. It’s going to move them
effortlessly into the vital connection.
There’s a couple of steps here. Step one is you want to reinforce the
promises that you have just made. Say them again. I know that
sounds redundant, but here’s how you do it.
You start with the word “remember.” “Remember” is a power word,
and “remember” is also a very powerful NLP term. It’s something that
takes the viewer’s mind and puts it into a state to where when you say
“remember,” the state of mind that you go into is a lot different than
when I just start talking, for example, or when I say, “Don’t forget.”
“Don’t forget” is a negative pattern. When I tell someone “don’t forget,”
the mind tends to look right past the negative words, and this has been
studied time and time again in psychology, and all you hear is the word
“forget.”
So, a lot of parents, when they learn this, they’re shocked, because
they’re telling their kids, “Don’t make a mess. Don’t leave your plate
there.” All the kid is hearing is, “Leave my plate here. Make a mess.”
They don’t hear the word “don’t.”
So, I use the word “remember.” “Keep your plate clean,” or whatever it
may be. “move your plate to the kitchen.” I’m giving them a positive
thing to do.
This is what you’re going to do when you’re reinforcing the promise to
your viewer. You’re going to say, “Remember. I’m about to share
benefits.” And you want to reword the benefits here. “I’m about to
share some killer stuff on losing weight with you, some tips that you’ve
never heard of before, little-known tips to help you see the result…, the
next part is the result, “to help you drop weight faster, feel better, and
get inside those skinny jeans.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

7
That’s all I’m doing here; reinforcing the same promises that I’ve
already made using slightly different words. That’s it. But the word
“remember” is the most important part of that.
And then step 2 is you want to open the nightmare story loop.
The nightmare story is the very first aspect of your vital connection.
You need to open the loop here.
So, you said, “Remember, I’m going to share all of this good stuff with
you that’s going to give you this result,” and step 2 comes in, “first.”
And that’s the word. You don’t use the word “but,” right? You can say
“However, first,” or “yet first.” I just start with the word “First, I’m going
to share a story with you that’s pretty embarrassing, and you’ll see why
in just a moment, so keep watching.”
See what I mean by that “keep watching” phrase? I’ve already
included it twice, and I’m probably 5 slides into my VSL.
Look at the pattern here. I’m opening a loop to the nightmare story
that you’ve already created. If you’ve done the homework, you’ve
already created your nightmare story and all I’m doing here is I’m
opening a loop that’s going to come back to those tips.
So, I say, “Remember, you’re going to discover all of this great stuff
that’s going to result in all of this great stuff. First,” so that’s the loop
break. Right here is where you start the loop.
So, they’re going, “Okay, tell me the good stuff, tell me the… Oh,
you’re going to tell me a story first.”
If you just say, “First, I’m going to share a story with you,” they might
go, “Ah, come on. Don’t tell me a story, just tell me the stuff. Give me
the stuff.” But if you embellish that with using power words and with
using some clever wordsmithing, by using the things that come natural
to a nightmare story, nightmare stories are, by their very nature,
embarrassing. They are scary. You can use any word that you want
here that fits a nightmare. I used “embarrassing,” because for me it
was.
So, “First, I’m going to share a story with you that’s pretty
embarrassing.” Now, you’ve tied into the human psyche. You’ve
really, really hit something powerful here. The human psyche really
wants to hear about that embarrassing stuff. If you doubt that, just look
at the readership of TMZ.com or reality shows, or gossip rag
magazines.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

8
I’m sorry to say, but the majority of people that you are going to be
talking to kind of get into gossip.
So, if you make your opening loop to your nightmare story a little bit
gossipy, like if you say, “I’m going to share a story that’s embarrassing.
In fact, it’s got one part in it that I almost edited out of this presentation
because it’s so bad,” people are going to be going, “Oh, you’ve got to
tell me this story.”
So, now, what have I done? I’ve implanted in their mind that they’re
going to receive all of these cool things, these benefits that are unique,
that are going to teach them how to do fill-in-the-blank, that’s going to
give them fill-in-the-blank results.
But now, I’ve moved them completely out of that, into rapport. “Tell me
your story.” I moved them temporarily out of, “What can you do for
me?” to, “Tell me more about you.”
Once you do that, the sale is almost in hand. As long as you connect
with them through the next section, which is the vital connection, as
long as you can make that vital connection, build that state of rapport,
at this point you’ve moved from the state of decision to the state of
desire. And that is your goal throughout the entire video sales letter
process.
It all starts with this one simple sentence: “First, I’m going to share a
story with you.” You just have to make that sentence a little bit more
juicy, by telling them that this story is really weird, it’s unique, it’s
perplexing.
You can use all of these words that we’ve given you in power words
and power phrases. Use those kind of words. Make it super-
intriguing, so they’re like, “Wow, I’ve really got to hear this story.” At
that point, they’re in rapport.
And finally, and this is the easiest part of the whole thing, you end with
one simple word. And this is what the agreement. “Deal?” That’s all
you say.
I’m going to go back here. “First, I’m going to share a story with you
that’s pretty embarrassing.” I can elaborate on that. I can say, “In fact,
there was one part in it that’s so embarrassing, my mother turned
blush red. Red as the table that I’m sitting at. You’ll see why in just a
moment, so keep watching. Deal?”
All I do is end in the word “deal.” Here’s how the whole thing works.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 9 --

9
“First, remember, I’m going to tell you all of this good stuff, and it’s
going to give you these good results. But first, I’m going to tell you a
story that’s a little bit funky and it’s a little bit embarrassing. Deal?”
What have I done here? I’ve made the user say, “yes.” If they watch
one second of me on this point, they’ve said yes, and that is what you
want to start having the viewer say to themselves, mentally.
You will see, in the coming sections, how often we use the yes
principle, how often we use the technique to get people to say “yes,” to
agree to an open-ended question.
You want to do this especially during your grand offer. That’s the key
step to do this.
But, here is where you start, and you just say, “Deal?” “Deal” is really
the best phrase I’ve come up with and it’s one word.
So, all we’re doing is entering into a slight agreement. When you enter
an agreement with somebody, it creates a different state. Your viewer
comes to the video sales letter expecting you just to sell them
something or, in some cases, just expecting you to tell them a bunch of
stuff for free.
But now, you’ve entered into a verbal agreement of sorts. Mentally
speaking, this is what’s going through the mind even if it’s not
conscious, even if it’s subconscious. Once you say “deal,” they go
“Sure. I want to hear this embarrassing story.”
You’ve just made a deal. What’s the power of that? You’re going to
make another deal when they buy your product.
So, they’ve already made the first deal, it’s a logical conclusion to go to
the second. That’s what makes this whole thing so powerful.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
